outline.title: Scaling to a Larger Project
outline.summary: Title slide. The framework is not limited to a single sprint. A large project is a sequence of focused sprints, each targeting a single moment in the user journey.
outline.image_plan: Use a suitable image if needed

outline.title: The problem with big projects
outline.summary: Large projects fail when the team tries to design everything at once. The solution is not a bigger sprint — it is a sequence of smaller, focused ones. One moment. One sprint. One decision.
outline.image_plan: Use a suitable image if needed

outline.title: Map the journey first, assign sprints second
outline.summary: Before running a single sprint, map the user's journey from first contact to the long-term goal. Each step in that journey is a candidate sprint. Sprint 000 is always first. After that, prioritise by risk — the riskiest, least-understood moment gets the next sprint.
outline.image_plan: Use a suitable image if needed

outline.title: A typical sprint sequence for a large project
outline.summary: Visual table showing a realistic sprint sequence: Sprint 000 Foundation, Sprint 001 Onboarding, Spike 01 (technical question), Sprint 002 Core Feature, Sprint 003 Notifications, Sprint 004+ onwards. Shows type, what it produces, and priority (Must/Should/Could).
outline.image_plan: Use a suitable image if needed

outline.title: Four rules for running sprints at scale
outline.summary: Rule 1: One moment per sprint — if you find two sprint questions about different moments, split it. Rule 2: The Brain compounds — each sprint makes the next one cheaper; by Sprint 005 the AI has deep context. Rule 3: Spikes run in parallel — a spike only blocks a sprint if the sprint depends on its answer. Rule 4: Dissent review triggers fire — overruled concerns from early sprints surface automatically when later sprints hit the relevant territory.
outline.image_plan: Use a suitable image if needed

outline.title: Choose the right format for each sprint
outline.summary: Four sprint formats: Full Sprint (Map→Sketch→Decide→Synthesise, half day, complex features), Lite Sprint (Map→Decide, 1-2 hours, refinements), Workshop (compressed, 2-3 hours, time-pressured), Spike (Qualify→Investigate→Recommend, 4 hours max, blocking technical questions).
outline.image_plan: Use a suitable image if needed

outline.title: The Brain gets smarter with every sprint
outline.summary: The three living documents (PRINCIPLES.md, PERSONAS.md, DECISIONS.md) accumulate knowledge across sprints. Sprint 003 pre-flights automatically read decisions from Sprints 001 and 002. By Sprint 005, the AI acts like a senior team member who has been on the project from day one. Compounding engineering — the work you do today reduces the cost of every future sprint.
outline.image_plan: Use a suitable image if needed

outline.title: Start small. Scale deliberately.
outline.summary: Closing slide. You don't need to plan every sprint upfront — plan the first 3 to 5 and let the backlog evolve. The sprint backlog is the roadmap. The definition of done is when all Must moments have a validated spec and the decisions are logged.
outline.image_plan: Use a suitable image if needed
